dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm EMT Jordan. What led you to call 911 today?
I have chest pain, weakness, fatigue-basically, I need you to take me to the ER.
Let's check if you know who we are. What is your name?
Carla. I want no part in answering questions. Just take me to the hospital.
Okay Carla. I'm EMT Jordan. Can you tell me your date of birth?
No! Just take me. I don't need to be asked questions.
Your airway is patent and you are breathing on your own.
Respirations: 18 and even. Carla, are you having trouble breathing?
Pulse oximeter was initially unable to read a value. We'll try another site now.
Pulse is 115, weak and regular.
Your skin is warm and dry. No cyanosis noted. Any new scars or rashes?
You mentioned fatigue. Any lightheadedness today?
You have a history of epilepsy, diabetes, and a personality disorder. Are you currently managing insulin on your own?
You said you were sleeping all day and couldn't eat. Last drink?
Correct, you take Lantus and Humalog regularly. Any chance those doses were missed?
Also, you have an allergy to Ultram. Is that correct?
You arrived at the door with shoes on the wrong feet and no lights on. Is that right?
Blood pressure is 86 over 54. Keep your arm steady for a moment.
We're checking your blood sugar now. You may feel a small prick.
Starting 4-lead monitoring for continuous heart assessment.
Given vitals and rising pulse, I'm activating the diabetic hyperglycemia protocol.
IV was attempted in left arm but unsuccessful. We tried due to PICC line history but couldn't secure access.
I see multiple linear scars, consistent with past IV lines, on your arms.
Head and neck appear atraumatic. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light (pearl).
Ears, nose, and throat look normal. Mucous membranes are pink and moist.
Neck is supple, no visible lymphadenopathy. You report no neck pain. Any swelling?
Chest has symmetrical expansion, and breath sounds are clear bilaterally.
No distension or tenderness found. No guarding or rigidity.
You report incontinence-specific type?
Strong pulses, sensation intact in all extremities. No deformity, angulation, or masses noted.
Glasgow Coma Scale 15-fully alert, but you seem very uncooperative, is that true?
Second reading: Blood pressure is 93 over 58, consistent with prior low but moderate rise.
SpO2 is now 100% after oxygen applied. Still 115 pulse-emotional state likely impacts.
Pulse increased to 120. Respirations are now 17-still no trouble breathing.
Long-term insulin-dependent diabetes, C.diff recently, and personality disorder. Any history of seizures?
You mention recent C.diff and a blood-borne infection-has that impacted appetite or mobility?
You stated you are unable to care for yourself. Who is supposed to provide those daily needs?
[COMPANY_NAME] it is-best for your ongoing management and history of complex needs.
You are being placed on the stretcher now. Please try to stay still.
No further IV attempts unless you're conscious and agree.
Uncooperative throughout assessment. Vitals improved but blood glucose still undetermined.
Vital signs and condition summary transmitted. Recent C.diff and infection noted.
Scars consistent with multiple IV lines. Noted as retrieval but documented.
All actions followed protocol. Vitals and EKG stable per protocol readings.
Final BP recorded at 101 over 61. Blood glucose remained undetermined through all checks.
Will hand off to hospital staff. Social services to be alerted per RN request.
IV access attempt failed-no fluids administered due to refusal and failed attempt.
Patient was aggressive and uncooperative. All care steps per protocol followed.
MD and hospital flagged for social services-caregiver missing, per patient complaint.
Continuous EKG monitoring initiated and did not show any significant deviance.
Last vitals: pulse 120, BP 101/61, SpO2 99%. Pulse ox improved markedly from initial 0.
She denied eating but claims it was because of sleep, not refusal of food.
All noted in EKG and treatment logged. Transport smooth and aligned with expectations.
No new interventions performed-purely under protocol and refusal of patient cooperation.
Patient currently stable though demanding. Social services to assist upon arrival.
Transport condition: uncooperative, complex care needs, psychiatric outline noted.
All data and suspected non-adherence flagged in documentation for hospital.
ED to be alerted about possible social services referral as caregiver is not at residence.
No trauma noted but active behavioral complaint.
ED has been directly informed with all critical updates.
Self-reported incontinence-liquid diarrhea-if this is chronic, hydration could be a concern.
GCS 15 maintained but hostility persistent throughout all steps and queries.
Due to diabetic and psychiatric co-morbidities, ED will aggressively evaluate adherence and hydration.
Initially low BP but no collapsing within vehicle parameters.
Transport urgent-updated as such for hospital triage protocols.
Patient's final handoff is complete. All forms are transmitted.
No worsening in transport. Otherwise stable with alertness and equilibrium maintained.
Self-reported incontinence-liquid diarrhea-if this is chronic, hydration could be a concern.
Please note if blood sugar issues worsen, even in mild form-ED will they need to step in.
Patient demands care but shows denial of capacity to manage basic needs. Social services will be key.
Likely handed to hospital's critical handler due to all access refusal.
Mandated reporting likely for absent caregiver. ED to assess and follow.
No rashes or swelling found. Skin otherwise normal.
IV initiation failed at left AC-no further attempts made. Patient refused.
Meds properly prescribed up through protocol sheet. No mishandling noted.
No HeEnt issues present. Not at immediate risk.
Patient claims help but rarely under it. Handoff to be performed accordingly.
GCS intact and no signs of impending collapse. Hostility still acute.
No crash symptoms now, but low BP a monitoring point for ED.
All steps done but limited by uncooperative nature and no sensory feedback.
[COMPANY_NAME] is still most appropriate and patient requested.
No depressive or anxious indicators technically observed but self-stated notes are clear.
Consistent. No stroke or tox suspected at this point.
Upon arrival, please inform ED of history and request social services as needed.
No family on arrival, and brother missing. As per patient claim.
All findings confirm you are awake and capable yet pushed for full support.
Social services will address reported dependency and possible neglect.
We asked for you to tell us all information-consider us as partners.
ED to act on your care within protocol as we did.
If concerns arise, please inform ED upon arrival.
Your complex behavioral and physical profiles are known to hospital on arrival.
AC IV attempt failed-recorded as such. Watch hydration and other symptoms.
Hospital is alerted. You will be thoroughly checked on arrival.
Any newly diagnosed conditions will be the hospital's responsibility.
In essence, not the most urgent physical case but one requiring full behavioral support.
BP and pulse are only mildly abnormal for now.
SpO2 saw improvement and BP trending after second cycle.
Heart rhythm remains tachy but stable.
No bad EKG readings in transport. 99% meets safety at this threshold.
